articles.author: Lisa Mascaro, Mary Clare Jalonick
articles.description: President Donald Trump says he will sign an order instructing the Homeland Security secretary to immediately pay Transportation Security Administration workers. The Senate is working overnight to resolve the 42-day funding impasse that has jammed airports and left workers without paychecks. Trump announced his decision in a social media post saying he wanted to quickly stop the “Chaos at the Airports.” The White House and senators appear to be closing in on a endgame before TSA workers miss another paycheck Friday. Democrats have been refusing to fund the Department of Homeland Security as they demand changes to rein in Trump’s immigration enforcement operations.
articles.title: Trump says he'll order pay for TSA agents as Senate works overnight

articles.author: Kate Brumback
articles.description: A federal judge is weighing Fulton County’s demand that the FBI return ballots and records from the 2020 election that were seized from an Atlanta-area warehouse. On Friday, the judge plans to hear arguments after court-ordered mediation failed. Fulton County said the seizure of ballots was improper and unconstitutional, while the FBI says it's investigating possible violations of the law. The FBI’s move was one of several actions by the Trump administration that have alarmed Democrats and many election officials who are concerned it is using law enforcement to pursue the president’s personal grievances and is planning ways to interfere in this year’s midterm elections.
articles.title: Judge to hear Fulton County's demand for return of seized 2020 ballots

articles.author: Fatima Hussein
articles.description: The Treasury Department is drawing up plans to print President Donald Trump’s signature on every new U.S. paper bill. Treasury announced the idea Thursday. Treasury says it would mark the first time a sitting president’s signature appears on new currency. The announcement is part of a broader push to put Trump’s name and image on public symbols. Treasury also backs a separate effort for a Trump coin. Earlier this month, a federal arts commission approves a design for a gold commemorative coin with his face.
articles.title: US Treasury working to put Trump's signature on new paper currency in first for sitting president

articles.author: Steve Karnowski
articles.description: Minnesota will be the flagship of the “No Kings” protest movement Saturday when Bruce Springsteen performs “Streets of Minneapolis” in a state where emotions are still raw over President Donald Trump’s immigration crackdown and the deaths of two residents who were shot by federal officers. The rally is part of a nationwide series of more than 3,100 planned events in communities large and small in all 50 states that are expected to draw millions of people. Organizers have designated the Minnesota rally, at the State Capitol in St. Paul, as Saturday’s flagship event. Rallies are also planned in more than a dozen other countries,
articles.title: Bruce Springsteen to lead 'No Kings' flagship protest in Minnesota

articles.author: Aamer Madhani
articles.description: Facing a convulsing stock market, President Donald Trump has moved to buy himself more time and hold off on carrying out a threat to obliterate Iran’s energy plants. Trump made the threat to try to force the Islamic Republic to let tankers through the Strait of Hormuz. At first, he gave Tehran 48 hours. Then he extended it to five days. On Thursday, he said he’ll now wait until April 6 because he said talks aimed at ending the conflict are going “very well. Iran has publicly insisted it is not negotiating with the White House. Trump made the announcement after U.S. markets closed out their worst day since the start of the war.
articles.title: Trump buys more time for talks, delays threat to bomb power plants

articles.author: Lorian Belanger
articles.description: Israel’s military says it launched strikes on Tehran early Friday while smoke was seen billowing in the Beirut skyline. President Trump has delayed his threat to obliterate Iran’s energy plants over its effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
articles.title: Live updates: Israel launches strikes on Tehran after Trump delays Strait of Hormuz deadline again

articles.author: Aniruddha Ghosal, Allan Olingo
articles.description: Farmers around the world are facing fertilizer shortages and rising costs after the war in Iran disrupted shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, which handles a third of global fertilizer trade. Rising natural gas prices are also driving up costs just as planting in the Northern Hemisphere begins. The poorest farmers in developing countries are likely to feel the squeeze first, threatening harvests and incomes. Higher fuel prices can ripple through processing, transport and delivery. Even if crops grow well, the delays and unstable oil market are expected to contribute to a spike in food prices.
articles.title: Fertilizer crisis hits farmers as Iran war disrupts supply

articles.author: Mohammad Anouti, Fadi Tawil
articles.description: Lebanon’s coastal southern city of Tyre — once famed for its Roman ruins and white sandy beaches — is a ghost town. Abandoned dogs roam vacant streets dotted with apartment buildings blasted by recent Israeli airstrikes. The ancient city emptied last week as Israel escalated its attacks against Shiite militant group Hezbollah and issued sweeping evacuation orders for all of southern Lebanon, forcing over 1 million people to flee. But several thousand residents linger. Some say they can't afford to abandon their work. Others have remained defiant, vowing to stay on their land as anxiety grows over Israel's intentions to occupy southern Lebanon.
articles.title: Few remain in Lebanon's Tyre after Israeli airstrikes

articles.author: Kostya Manenkov, Stefanie Dazio
articles.description: Scientists are learning to dive beneath polar ice and study the study the flora and fauna below. The Finnish Scientific Diving Academy has designed a program in northern Finland for participants to venture below the thick Arctic and Antarctic ice for research. The Arctic is warming four times faster than the rest of the planet. So scientists need to study what’s underneath the remaining Arctic and Antarctic ice. But carrying out such research requires specialized scuba diving skills plus the proper scientific background. And those are qualifications only a few hundred people in the world currently have.
articles.title: Diving class in Finland's north trains next generation of polar ice researchers

articles.author: Suman Naishadham
articles.description: A young Spanish woman died in Barcelona after winning a long court fight for her euthanasia right. On Thursday, Noelia Castillo received life-ending medicine after Spain's high court denied her family's appeal. She was 25. In 2024, a Catalan review panel approved her application and deemed her suffering severe and incurable. Her father challenged the decision and said mental illness rendered her unfit to make the decision. Various levels of courts rejected the appeal, including Spain’s Supreme Court in January. A European rights court also turned the case away in March. In a TV interview on Wednesday, Castillo said she wanted peace and felt misunderstood.
articles.title: Spanish woman dies by legal euthanasia in case that drew national attention
